title: Demand and Supply
summary:
## The big idea Almost everything in microeconomics comes back to demand and supply. Once you can confidently move these two curves around and read off what happens to price and quantity, then elasticities, taxes, subsidies, price controls and even the big macro model all become the same skill in a new costume. So it is worth taking this chapter slowly. A **market** is simply any arrangement that brings **buyers** and **sellers** together — it doesn't need a physical place (think of the global oil market, or eBay). In a **free market** nobody sets the price on purpose. Instead the price *emerges* from the tug-of-war between what buyers want and what sellers offer. That self-organising process is called the **price mechanism**, and it quietly does three jobs at once whenever a good becomes scarcer or more popular: it **rations** the good (a higher price puts off some buyers so the limited supply goes to those willing to pay), it **signals** to producers where resources are wanted (a high price shouts ‘bring more here’), and it gives producers an **incentive** to respond (higher price means higher profit). Adam Smith called this the ‘invisible hand’: no planner is in charge, yet resources still flow to where people want them. ## Demand: the buyers' side **Demand** is the quantity buyers are **willing *and* able to buy** at each price over a period of time. Both words matter — wanting a sports car isn't demand unless you can actually pay for it, which is why economists say *effective* demand rather than mere desire. The **law of demand** says that as a good's price rises, the quantity demanded falls (and vice versa), all else equal. The demand curve therefore slopes **downwards**. But don't just memorise that — there are three real reasons behind it. First, the **substitution effect**: when a good gets pricier, rival goods look cheaper by comparison, so some buyers switch away from it. Second, the **income effect**: a price rise makes your money go less far, so your real spending power falls and you can afford less. Third, **diminishing marginal utility**: each extra unit you consume gives a little less satisfaction than the one before (the first slice of pizza is bliss, the fifth is a chore), so you'll only buy those later units if the price drops. ### The one distinction that wins marks: movement vs shift This trips up more students than anything else, so be strict about it. A change in **the good's own price** moves you *along* the demand curve — we call it an **extension** if quantity rises and a **contraction** if it falls. Nothing else changed; you simply slid to a new point on the *same* curve. A change in **any other factor** shifts the **whole curve** left or right, because now buyers want a different amount at *every* price. > Golden rule: a good's **own price can never shift its own curve**. Price sits on the axis, so a change in it is always a movement along. The things that *do* shift demand are the **conditions of demand** — remember them with **PIRATES**: **P**opulation, **I**ncome (for a *normal* good more income shifts demand right; for an *inferior* good like bus travel it shifts left as people trade up), **R**elated goods (a dearer *substitute* shifts this good's demand right; a dearer *complement* shifts it left), **A**dvertising and tastes, **T**axes/subsidies on buyers, **E**xpectations of future prices, and **S**easons. ## Supply: the sellers' side **Supply** is the quantity producers are willing and able to sell at each price. The **law of supply** says that as price rises, quantity supplied rises, so the supply curve slopes **upwards**. Why? A higher price means more potential **profit** (an incentive to produce more), and as firms push output up their **marginal cost** tends to rise, so they need a higher price to make each extra unit worthwhile. The **conditions of supply** — what shifts the curve — are captured by **PINTSWC**: **P**roductivity, **I**ndirect taxes (shift supply left), **N**umber of firms, **T**echnology, **S**ubsidies (shift supply right), **W**eather/shocks, and **C**osts of production (wages, raw materials, energy). Same discipline as before: the good's own price is a movement along supply; everything else shifts the whole curve. ## Putting them together: equilibrium **Equilibrium** is the price where the plans of buyers and sellers match exactly — quantity demanded equals quantity supplied and the market ‘clears’. There is no pressure to change, which is why it's called equilibrium. On a diagram it's simply where the two curves cross.
**What each part means:** the **vertical axis is Price**, the **horizontal axis is Quantity**. The **red line (D)** is demand — it slopes down because a lower price means more is bought. The **yellow line (S)** is supply — it slopes up because a higher price means more is produced. Where they cross is the **equilibrium**: **P\*** (read across to the price axis) is the market-clearing price and **Q\*** (read down to the quantity axis) is the quantity traded. At P\*, the amount buyers want exactly equals the amount sellers offer.
### How the market actually reaches equilibrium The clever part is that the market *self-corrects*. If the price is set **too high** (above P\*), sellers offer more than buyers want, leaving a **surplus** of unsold stock — so sellers cut prices until it clears. If the price is **too low** (below P\*), buyers want more than is available, creating a **shortage** — so buyers bid the price up. Either way the price is pushed back to P\*. You can see this in a simple schedule: | Price ($) | Quantity demanded | Quantity supplied | |---|---|---| | 6 | 90 | 40 | | 7 | 80 | 60 | | **8** | **70** | **70** | | 9 | 60 | 80 | | 10 | 50 | 100 | At \$6 demand (90) beats supply (40): a shortage of 50 pushes price up. At \$10 supply (100) beats demand (50): a surplus of 50 pushes price down. Only at **\$8** do the two match at **70 units** — that's the equilibrium. ## When a curve shifts: a worked example Suppose a successful advertising campaign hits the electric-car market *while* battery costs fall at the same time. Work it through in steps. The advertising improves tastes, so **demand shifts right** (D1 → D2); on its own that raises both price and quantity. The cheaper batteries lower costs, so **supply shifts right** too; on its own that lowers price but raises quantity. Combining them, **quantity clearly rises** (both effects push it up), but the effect on **price is ambiguous** — demand pushes it up while supply pushes it down, so the outcome depends on which shift is bigger. Saying exactly that earns the top evaluation marks. The diagram below shows just the demand-increase half:
**What each part means:** supply **(S, yellow)** stays put. Demand shifts right from **D1 (dark red)** to **D2 (bright red)** — at every price, more is now wanted. The equilibrium moves up the supply curve from the first crossing to the second. Reading off the axes: price rises from **P1 to P2** and quantity rises from **Q1 to Q2**. So an increase in demand raises *both* price and quantity. (A fall in demand would shift the curve left and lower both.)
## Common mistakes to avoid Writing ‘demand goes up’ when it's really the good's own price changing — that's an *extension* (a movement), not a rise in demand. Drawing a shift when you mean a movement, or vice versa (the examiner reads your diagram as your answer). Forgetting that when **both** curves move you can usually be sure of only *one* of price or quantity. And muddling substitutes with complements, or normal with inferior goods, when you apply PIRATES. ## Real-world application The 2022 jump in global **wheat and energy prices** (Russia–Ukraine war) shifted the supply of food and fuel **left** worldwide, raising prices. Young, growing populations in **India and Nigeria** keep the demand for housing and food shifting **right**, while ageing **Japan** sees some demands fall. And the **oil market** shows both curves moving together — OPEC+ supply cuts alongside reviving **Chinese** demand push price up but leave the change in quantity ambiguous.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Scarcity → markets → the price mechanism**, which *rations*, *signals* and *incentivises* (Adam Smith's ‘invisible hand’). - **Demand** slopes down because of the **substitution effect, income effect and diminishing marginal utility**. - **Supply** slopes up because higher prices mean higher **profit** and cover rising **marginal cost**. - **Own price change → movement along** (extension/contraction). **Any other factor → the whole curve shifts.** - Demand shifters = **PIRATES**; supply shifters = **PINTSWC**. - **Equilibrium** is where D = S (P\*, Q\*); above it → **surplus** (price falls), below it → **shortage** (price rises). - When **both** curves shift, you can sign only price *or* quantity — the other is **indeterminate**.

keyTerms.definition: Any arrangement bringing buyers and sellers together to trade; it need not be a physical place.
How prices ration, signal and incentivise to allocate resources in a free market (the ‘invisible hand’).
Demand backed by the willingness AND ability to pay, not mere desire.
As a good's price rises, its quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus.
As a good's price rises, buyers switch to now-relatively-cheaper alternatives, lowering quantity demanded.
A price change alters real purchasing power, changing how much of the good buyers can afford.
A movement along the demand curve caused by a change in the good's own price.
A normal good's demand rises with income; an inferior good's demand falls as income rises.
Substitutes are consumed instead of each other; complements are consumed together.
As price rises, quantity supplied rises, ceteris paribus, due to higher profit and rising marginal cost.
The market-clearing price where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
When price is below equilibrium so quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, pushing price up.
When price is above equilibrium so quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, pushing price down.

diagrams: **Basic equilibrium (Fig 1):** Price on the vertical axis, Quantity on the horizontal; downward D, upward S. Mark P* and Q* with dashed lines from the crossing point. Always label axes, both curves and the equilibrium.
**Demand shift (Fig 2):** keep S fixed and shift demand D1 → D2; show the equilibrium sliding up the supply curve and mark the new P2 and Q2. A leftward shift (fall in demand) lowers both.

**Shortage & surplus:** draw a price line above equilibrium (label the horizontal gap ‘surplus / excess supply’) and one below (‘shortage / excess demand’), with arrows for the direction price then moves.
**Both curves shift:** move D and S right together to show quantity clearly rising while the change in price is ambiguous — the classic evaluation diagram.
tips: **Label every diagram fully** — axes (Price, Quantity), all curves (D, S, D1, D2) and the equilibrium price & quantity. The examiner marks your diagram as part of the answer.
Use exact wording: *‘demand shifts right from D1 to D2’*, not ‘demand goes up’. **Shift vs movement** is the single most tested distinction.
For ‘analyse/explain’, **chain the logic**: cause → which curve shifts and why → new equilibrium → effect on price AND quantity.
Beware the **own-price trap**: a change in a good's own price is always a movement along its own curve, never a shift.
When **both curves shift**, state the effect on price *or* quantity is **indeterminate** and explain it depends on the relative size of the shifts.
Practise **reading a schedule**: compare Qd and Qs at each price to spot shortages, surpluses and the equilibrium.
Bring in **varied real examples** (global wheat/oil shocks, population growth in India/Nigeria) for application marks.
